metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = allowed transitions (Δℓ=±1); CRIMSON = forbidden transitions (Δℓ=0)

beats.narration_text: Hydrogen's 2p state decays in about a billionth of a second. The 2s state, barely different in energy, is stuck for a tenth of a second — a hundred million times longer. Same atom, nearly the same energy. One decays instantly. The other waits.

The difference isn't the energy gap. It's the shape of the orbital. The 2p state is pear-shaped — it has angular momentum. The 2s state is round — it has none. And the photon it would need to carry the energy away requires angular momentum to exist.

A photon must carry exactly one unit of angular momentum. The 2s state has none. The 1s state has none. If you try to go 2s to 1s, where does the angular momentum go? The transition is forbidden — single-photon emission simply cannot happen. Why?

props.headline: A photon must carry exactly one unit of angular momentum. The…

card.headline: 2s → 1s: photon forbidden. 2p → 1s: instant. Why?
card.dek: A photon carries one unit of angular momentum. Where does it go when none is available?

beats.narration_text: First, recall the orbital labels. s orbitals have zero orbital angular momentum. p orbitals have one unit. d orbitals have two. The letter encodes the quantum number ℓ: s means ℓ equals zero, p means ℓ equals one.

props.headline: First, recall the orbital labels. s orbitals have zero orbital angular…

beats.narration_text: A photon is a spin-one particle. It carries exactly one unit of angular momentum. When an atom emits a photon, total angular momentum must be conserved — what the atom loses, the photon carries away.
So the atom's angular momentum must change by exactly one unit when it emits a photon. The allowed transitions satisfy delta-ℓ equals plus or minus one. That is the electric-dipole selection rule.

For the 2p to 1s transition: ℓ changes from one to zero — delta-ℓ equals negative one. Allowed. The photon carries away one unit of angular momentum. Conservation is satisfied. The transition happens fast.

For the 2s to 1s transition: ℓ stays zero — delta-ℓ equals zero. Forbidden. A single photon cannot carry away zero angular momentum and still be a photon. The transition has no allowed single-photon path.

The parity argument makes this precise. Under parity — reflecting all coordinates — s orbitals are even and p orbitals are odd. The electric dipole operator is odd. The integral of even times odd times even is zero. Only an even-to-odd or odd-to-even transition gives a nonzero overlap.
Forbidden means slow, not impossible. The 2s state can still decay — but only by emitting two photons simultaneously, each carrying part of the angular momentum. That process is rare.

props.headline: Forbidden means slow, not impossible. The 2s state can still decay…

card.headline: Δℓ = ±1: allowed (fast). Δℓ = 0: E1 forbidden (slow).
card.dek: Angular momentum and parity must both be conserved. The selection rule follows.

beats.narration_text: These selection rules pattern the whole hydrogen spectrum. Every allowed spectral line corresponds to a delta-ℓ of plus or minus one. The Lyman series, the Balmer series, the Paschen series — they're all built of transitions that satisfy this one rule.

The 21-centimeter line — the radio signal used to map galaxies across the universe — comes from a magnetic-dipole transition in hydrogen that is also forbidden by the electric-dipole rule. Its lifetime is ten million years. The universe is big enough to produce enough of them to see.

Illustrative: hydrogen 2p to 1s, delta-ℓ equals minus one, allowed — lifetime 1.6 nanoseconds. Hydrogen 2s to 1s, delta-ℓ equals zero, electric-dipole forbidden — lifetime 0.12 seconds, eight orders of magnitude longer. And the 21-centimeter line: delta-ℓ equals zero, magnetic dipole, lifetime ten million years — visible across the observable universe.

The question: why can't hydrogen's 2s state emit a photon the way 2p can? Because a photon carries one unit of angular momentum, and a 2s to 1s jump changes the orbital angular momentum by zero. Conservation is violated. One selection rule — delta-ℓ equals plus or minus one — determines which transitions are fast and which are forbidden. Quantum mechanics.

props.headline: The question: why can't hydrogen's 2s state emit a photon the…

card.headline: Δℓ = ±1 only. Angular momentum conservation forbids the rest.
card.dek: A photon carries spin 1. If Δℓ = 0, there is nowhere for that spin to go.
